title: SEGMENT 18 — The frontier — radioligands, immunostimulatory payloads, AI design

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — The frontier — radioligands, immunostimulatory payloads, AI design
Let me close with a tour of the frontier.
Where is ADC technology heading?
What are the next ten years going to look like?
I'll give you four directions that I think are most likely to define the next era.
The first is radioligand therapy, which is not technically an ADC but is conceptually adjacent.
Radioligands are targeted molecules — sometimes antibodies, sometimes small peptides — conjugated to a radioactive isotope instead of a cytotoxic small molecule.
The radioactive atom decays inside or near the tumor cell and emits ionizing radiation that damages DNA at short range.
The most famous example is lutetium-177-PSMA-617, marketed as Pluvicto, approved in 2022 for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer.
Pluvicto is not an ADC — it uses a small peptide rather than an antibody — but the principle is the same: targeted delivery of a toxic payload to a defined cellular marker.
Radioligand therapy is exploding right now.
Novartis has built a major franchise around Pluvicto and a similar drug, Lutathera, for neuroendocrine tumors.
Several companies are developing radioligands against new targets.
The next frontier is alpha-emitters — radioisotopes like actinium-225 that emit higher-energy radiation over shorter distances, which may be more effective and have less off-target toxicity.
Alpha-emitter radioligands are in early clinical development now and may represent the next major wave.
The second direction is immunostimulatory payloads.
Instead of using a cytotoxic small molecule as the payload, you use a molecule that activates the immune system at the tumor site.
The classic example is a TLR7 or TLR8 agonist — small molecules that bind Toll-like receptors on immune cells and trigger an inflammatory response.

As an ADC payload, a TLR agonist gets delivered to the tumor environment, activates local immune cells, and turns a "cold" tumor (one the immune system is ignoring) into a "hot" tumor (one the immune system attacks).
Several immunostimulatory ADCs are in early clinical trials.
Bolt Biotherapeutics, Mersana, and others have programs in this space.
The clinical data is still early, but the concept is compelling — and it represents a fundamentally different mode of action from traditional cytotoxic ADCs.
Instead of just killing the cancer cells the antibody binds, you're recruiting the patient's own immune system to attack the tumor more broadly.
The third direction is AI-driven design.
The combinatorial space of ADCs — antibody times target times payload times linker times conjugation site — is enormous.
Hundreds of antibody targets.
Dozens of payload classes.
Multiple linker chemistries.
Multiple conjugation strategies.
Multiple DARs.
The number of possible ADC designs is far larger than any human team could explore systematically.
Machine learning models are beginning to be applied to predict which combinations will have the best therapeutic windows.
Several startups — Absci, Generate Biomedicines, Lila Sciences, Xaira Therapeutics, and others — are working on computational approaches to antibody design and ADC design.

The promise is that you can specify a target and a desired profile and then use AI to generate candidate molecules that hit the target with the right affinity, the right specificity, and the right physical properties for conjugation.
We're not there yet.
But progress over the next five years could meaningfully accelerate the design cycle.
The fourth direction is modular toolkit thinking.
This is less a specific technology than a way of approaching the field.
The mature ADC paradigm treats the antibody, the linker, the payload, and the conjugation chemistry as four independent modules that can be mixed and matched.
Pick an antibody for the target.
Pick a payload for the cancer biology.
Pick a linker for the desired bystander effect and stability.
Pick a conjugation strategy for the desired DAR profile and homogeneity.
Here's the analogy.
The future of ADCs looks like a modular toolkit — antibody by payload by linker by conjugation — where the designer assembles the right combination for each clinical situation.
The first generation of ADCs was characterized by one-off designs — Mylotarg, Adcetris, T-DM1 each built somewhat from scratch.
The current generation is dominated by platforms — the Seagen MMAE-vc platform, the Daiichi DXd platform, the various PBD platforms.
The next generation will likely be characterized by even more modular composability, where new ADCs can be designed and tested with much less custom chemistry per program.
If this modular vision plays out, the rate at which new ADCs reach clinical trials will accelerate substantially.

And the antibody-target landscape will broaden — antibodies that were not previously good targets, because they had poor internalization or low expression, may become tractable with the right payload and linker choices.
ADCs may become applicable to indications well beyond oncology — autoimmune diseases, fibrotic diseases, even infectious diseases.
Let me close this segment, and this lecture, with a reflection on where we are.
Twenty-five years after Mylotarg's first approval, ADCs have gone from a fringe idea to a central pillar of oncology.
T-DXd alone is reshaping breast cancer treatment for hundreds of thousands of patients a year.
A dozen ADCs are approved.
Hundreds are in clinical trials.
The industrial activity is enormous, the molecular toolkit is expanding, and the clinical results in many indications are genuinely transformative.
The concept — antibody plus linker plus payload — is simple enough to describe on a napkin.
The execution requires mastery of antibody engineering, organic chemistry, cellular pharmacology, manufacturing science, and clinical management.
The field is the culmination of decades of biological understanding and chemical engineering, and the best chapters are probably still ahead.

If you walked in here two hours ago with no idea what an ADC was, and you now have an intuition for what they are, why they work, why they sometimes fail, and what the frontier looks like — you have a framework you can use for the next twenty years of oncology news.
Because ADCs are not a passing trend.

They are a permanent addition to the toolkit, and the developments coming out of the labs at Daiichi, Seagen, ADC Therapeutics, AstraZeneca, and dozens of smaller companies will be reshaping cancer care for the foreseeable future.
Thank you.
Take a breath.
Questions?
